topics.title: Amazon ElastiCache — Redis Caching Patterns, Data Structures & Eviction
topics.description: ElastiCache is AWS's managed in-memory caching service for Redis and Memcached. Redis supports rich data structures (String, List, Set, Hash, Sorted Set), pub/sub messaging, TTL-based expiry, Lua scripting, and optional persistence. Cache-aside (look-aside) is the dominant pattern: app checks cache first, on miss loads from DB and writes to cache with TTL. Eviction policies (allkeys-lru, volatile-lru, allkeys-lfu) control what Redis removes when memory is full. Redis Cluster shards data across 3+ nodes for horizontal scaling. ElastiCache is critical for session storage, rate limiting, real-time leaderboards, and reducing database read load by 10–100×.
topics.story: Your database is a slow librarian who walks to a back room for every question. Redis is a sticky-note board at the front desk: 'user:123 → {name: Alice, xp: 1200}' written on a note with an expiry time. First request: the librarian walks, you write the note. Every request after reads the note in microseconds. When the note expires (TTL), the next request pays the librarian fee again and refreshes the note. If memory runs out, Redis silently removes the least recently used note (LRU eviction) — it's not a database, it's a fast-path shortcut.
topics.analogy: Cache-aside = check sticky note first (GET) → miss → fetch from DB → write to cache (SET with EX) → return. Write-through = write to cache AND DB simultaneously (consistent but adds write latency). TTL = note expiry (SET key value EX 3600 = 1 hour). Eviction: allkeys-lru = evict least recently used any key when memory full. volatile-lru = only evict keys that have a TTL set. noeviction = reject writes when full (use for session store — never lose data). Sorted Set (ZAGG) = leaderboard: ZADD board 1200 user:1 adds score, ZREVRANK board user:1 gets rank.
topics.memoryAnchor: Cache-aside: GET → miss → DB → SET TTL → return. Write-through: write cache+DB together. Eviction policies: allkeys-lru (most common), volatile-lru (only TTL keys), noeviction (sessions). Data structures: String (counters), List (queues), Set (unique members), Hash (objects), Sorted Set (leaderboards, rate limits). TTL: SET key val EX 3600. Redis Cluster: 16384 hash slots across 3+ nodes. Pub/Sub: PUBLISH channel msg, SUBSCRIBE channel. Pipelining: batch multiple commands = 1 round trip.
topics.firstPrinciples: Cache invalidation is the hardest problem in distributed systems. TTL-based expiry is the simplest strategy — data may be stale for up to TTL seconds (acceptable for non-critical reads: topic content, user profiles). Write-through avoids staleness but adds write latency and coupling. Cache-aside can serve stale data but decouples cache from DB. For write-heavy data, cache adds overhead without benefit. Redis is single-threaded for command processing — no lock contention, predictable latency, but one CPU core maximum for command throughput. Use pipelining or Lua scripts for multi-command atomicity.
topics.bruteForce: Query the database on every HTTP request. Under 100 concurrent users it works fine. At 1000 req/s with a 50ms DB query = 1000 DB connections all busy. DB becomes the bottleneck and application throughput plateaus.
topics.optimizedApproach: Spring @Cacheable auto-implements cache-aside: checks cache → miss → calls method → stores result with TTL. @CacheEvict on write methods to invalidate stale entries. Redis Sorted Set for real-time leaderboards (ZADD O(log N), ZREVRANK O(log N)). Rate limiting: INCR + EXPIRE per user per minute window. Session storage: HSET session:{token} field value, EXPIRE session:{token} 1800.
topics.whenToUse: Session store: always Redis (sub-ms, TTL, cluster HA). Read-heavy DB data (profiles, config, topics): cache-aside with 5–15 min TTL. Leaderboards and ranking: Sorted Set. Rate limiting per user: INCR + EXPIRE. Real-time messaging fan-out: pub/sub. NOT suitable for: data requiring strong consistency, write-heavy data (invalidation overhead > benefit), complex joins or aggregations.

examples.title: Spring Boot @Cacheable + @CacheEvict with Redis
examples.explanation: @Cacheable is declarative cache-aside: Spring checks the cache before calling the method. Cache key defaults to the method parameters — here 'topicContent::42'. On cache miss, the method runs and the result is stored. @CacheEvict removes the specific key on update, preventing stale reads. Per-cache TTL configuration ensures user XP (volatile) expires faster than topic content (stable). Redis key prefix 'devlearn:' namespaces entries to avoid collisions when multiple apps share one Redis cluster.
examples.realWorldUse: DevLearner: topic content is read thousands of times but updated rarely — 6-hour TTL gives huge DB relief. User XP is shown on every page but updates on every submission — 1-minute TTL means at worst 1-minute stale, but reduces 1000× DB hits. Session tokens stored in Redis with @CacheEvict on logout. For the leaderboard, use Redis Sorted Set directly (not @Cacheable) for real-time accuracy.

examples.title: Redis Sorted Set — Real-Time Leaderboard with ZADD & ZREVRANK
examples.explanation: Sorted Set stores (member, score) pairs. All operations are O(log N) using a skip list internally. ZADD replaces the score if the member already exists — idempotent upsert. ZINCRBY atomically adds to the current score (safe for concurrent XP updates without read-modify-write). ZREVRANK gives 0-indexed rank from highest score. The rate limiter uses a minute-window key: 'ratelimit:123:28046400' (userId:minuteEpoch). INCR is atomic — no race condition. Setting TTL only when count==1 means the window expires exactly 60s after the first request.
examples.realWorldUse: DevLearner leaderboard: ZADD on every accepted submission (XP earned). Top 10 rendered from ZREVRANGE — single Redis call instead of ORDER BY XP LIMIT 10 on submissions table (which requires full scan). Rate limiter: 10 code executions per minute per user — same pattern as the Redis-based rate limiting in production.

problems.title: Cache-Aside Pattern Implementation
problems.description: Implement a CacheAsideStore<K,V> that wraps a slow data source (simulated with a loader function). get(key) checks the in-memory cache first; on miss, calls the loader, stores the result with a maxSize eviction limit (evict oldest entry when full), and returns it. Track cache hits and misses.
problems.hint1: Use LinkedHashMap with accessOrder=true as the cache. Override removeEldestEntry to cap size.
problems.hint2: In get(): if cache.containsKey(key) → hit, return. Else: call loader, put in cache → miss.
problems.hint3: Track hits and misses as AtomicInteger fields. hitRate() = hits / (hits + misses).
problems.testCases:
DB load: alice
ALICE
ALICE
DB load: bob
BOB
ALICE
hits=3 misses=2 rate=60%

problems.title: Redis Sorted Set — Leaderboard Rank Operations
problems.description: Implement an in-memory Leaderboard using TreeMap as a mock Redis Sorted Set. Support: addScore(user, score), getScore(user), getRank(user) (1-indexed, highest first), getTopN(n) returns list of 'rank. user: score', and incrementScore(user, delta).
problems.hint1: Use a HashMap<String,Double> for scores and a TreeMap<Double, TreeSet<String>> for sorted ranking (descending).
problems.hint2: On addScore/increment: remove old score from TreeMap, update HashMap, insert new score into TreeMap.
problems.hint3: getRank: iterate TreeMap in descending order (descendingMap()) counting until you find the user.
problems.testCases:
Bob rank: 1
Alice rank: 2
Alice score: 1600
#1. bob: 2500
#2. alice: 1600
#3. carol: 800

problems.title: Sliding Window Rate Limiter
problems.description: Implement a sliding-window rate limiter using a LinkedList as a mock Redis sorted set. isAllowed(userId, maxRequests, windowMs) returns true if the user has fewer than maxRequests in the last windowMs milliseconds. Expired entries (outside the window) must be removed on every call.
problems.pattern: Sliding window, rate limiting, sorted set
problems.hint1: Store timestamps per userId: Map<String, LinkedList<Long>>. Each call adds current time to the list.
problems.hint2: Before checking the count, remove all timestamps older than (now - windowMs) from the front of the list.
problems.hint3: After pruning, list.size() < maxRequests → allow + add timestamp. Otherwise → deny.

topics.title: AWS Storage — EBS vs EFS vs Instance Store vs S3
topics.description: AWS offers four storage types with fundamentally different characteristics. EBS (Elastic Block Store): network-attached block storage for a single EC2 instance, survives stop/start, gp3 (16K IOPS max) for general use, io2 for high-IOPS databases. EFS (Elastic File System): POSIX NFS shared across multiple EC2s simultaneously, auto-scales, 10× more expensive than EBS, best for shared content. Instance Store: physically attached ephemeral storage — highest IOPS, sub-millisecond latency, data lost on stop/terminate. S3: object storage via REST API, unlimited scale, cheapest per GB, lifecycle policies for automatic tier transitions (Standard → IA → Glacier).
topics.story: EBS is an external hard drive plugged into your laptop — only you use it, but if you shut down and restart, all files remain. EFS is a Google Drive folder mounted on every EC2 simultaneously — all servers see the same files. Instance Store is your laptop's built-in SSD — blazing fast, but when you shut down the laptop, the SSD goes with it (ephemeral). S3 is a warehouse you access by mailing requests (REST API calls) — you can't 'mount' it, but it holds petabytes for pennies per GB per month.
topics.analogy: EBS = exclusive hard drive (1 EC2 owner, high performance, persistent, snapshots to S3). EFS = shared NFS (multi-EC2, POSIX, auto-scales, expensive). Instance Store = local SSD (fastest, lost on stop). S3 = object store (REST GET/PUT, unlimited, cheapest, lifecycle policies). EBS gp3: 3000 IOPS + 125 MB/s baseline, up to 16000 IOPS extra cost. io2: up to 64000 IOPS for high-IOPS DBs. EFS Performance Mode: General Purpose (default, low latency) vs Max I/O (high throughput, higher latency). S3 storage classes: Standard → Standard-IA (after 30d) → Glacier Instant (90d) → Glacier Deep Archive (365d).
topics.memoryAnchor: EBS: 1 EC2 at a time, persistent, snapshots, gp3 general / io2 high-IOPS. EFS: shared NFS, multiple EC2, auto-scales, 10× EBS cost. Instance Store: ephemeral, fastest, data lost on stop. S3: object, REST, unlimited, cheapest, lifecycle. EBS Multi-Attach: io1/io2 can attach to 16 Nitro instances (cluster FS). EBS snapshot: consistent backup copied to S3. S3 lifecycle: automate tier transitions by age.
topics.firstPrinciples: Block storage (EBS, Instance Store) presents raw bytes — OS formats the filesystem. File storage (EFS) presents a pre-formatted filesystem via NFS protocol. Object storage (S3) has no filesystem — store/retrieve named objects via HTTP. Choose block for DB workloads (OS-level control, consistent IOPS), file for shared application data across multiple hosts, object for large-scale archiving/backups/static assets. Instance Store throughput matches NVMe speeds (millions of IOPS) because there is no network hop — data is on the physical host. EBS must cross a network link even though latency is typically < 1ms.
topics.bruteForce: Store everything on S3 and read/write via API for every operation. 50ms round-trip per read makes this catastrophic for a database. Put all shared web content on each EC2's local disk — when new servers join, must sync content manually.
topics.optimizedApproach: Production DB: EBS io2 for data dir, gp3 for OS. Shared web static content (images, uploads): EFS mounted on all app servers. ML training datasets: EFS for shared read, S3 for checkpoint storage. Spark temp data: Instance Store (ephemeral is fine — re-compute on failure). Static assets, backups, logs: S3 with lifecycle to IA/Glacier. Log all EBS usage with CloudWatch: VolumeReadOps, VolumeWriteOps, BurstBalance.
topics.whenToUse: EBS gp3: OS disk, Docker volumes, general-purpose DB data. EBS io2: high-IOPS OLTP databases (Oracle, MySQL, Cassandra, high-write Kafka). EFS: shared web content, multi-AZ shared state for containers, Kubernetes ReadWriteMany PVs, ML training datasets. Instance Store: Spark executors, Cassandra/Kafka data nodes (use their own replication), distributed cache nodes. S3: media, logs, model artifacts, data lake, static website, backups, DR archives.

examples.title: EBS Volume Configuration & Snapshot Lifecycle
examples.explanation: gp3 decoupled IOPS/throughput from size: you can have a 100GB volume with 16000 IOPS (not possible with gp2 which capped IOPS at 3×size). This means you no longer need to over-provision size to get IOPS. io2 Block Express supports up to 64000 IOPS and is designed for Oracle, SQL Server, and high-write Cassandra/Kafka. EBS Multi-Attach (io1/io2 only) allows up to 16 Nitro instances to share a volume simultaneously — used for clustered filesystems like GFS2 or Oracle RAC.
examples.realWorldUse: DevLearner production DB: 500GB gp3 with 6000 IOPS. Daily snapshots via DLM retained for 7 days (point-in-time recovery to any day). Snapshot is consistent when taken while instance is stopped, or use --no-reboot for live snapshot (slight data inconsistency risk for DBs — better to use mysqldump for logical consistent backup alongside physical snapshot).

examples.title: S3 Lifecycle Policy — Automatic Tier Transitions
examples.explanation: Lifecycle transitions are the primary S3 cost optimization: a 10TB log archive moved to Glacier Deep Archive costs $9.90/month vs $230/month in Standard (23× cheaper). Intelligent-Tiering monitors access patterns and auto-moves objects — ideal when access pattern is unknown. The retrieval fee for IA/Glacier means frequent accesses are more expensive — always model total cost (storage + retrieval × frequency). S3 Replication (CRR/SRR) copies objects to another bucket — use for cross-region DR or compliance.
examples.realWorldUse: DevLearner: application logs → S3 Standard (first 30 days for debugging), Standard-IA (30-90 days), Glacier (90+ days, compliance). User-uploaded code submissions → Standard (30 days), then IA. Backup dumps → Standard-IA immediately (infrequent access, but recovery must be fast). DB snapshots → kept 7 days in Standard, then deleted.

problems.title: Storage Type Selector
problems.description: Implement a StorageSelector that maps a use case description to the optimal AWS storage type. Support use cases: high-iops-database, shared-web-content, spark-temp-data, static-assets, os-disk, ml-training-shared, compliance-archive.
problems.hint1: Use a switch expression or Map<String, StorageType> lookup.
problems.hint2: high-iops-database → EBS_IO2, shared-web → EFS, spark temp → INSTANCE_STORE, s3 for static/archive.
problems.hint3: ml-training-shared needs multiple EC2 read access → EFS.
problems.testCases:
EBS_IO2
EFS
INSTANCE_STORE
S3_STANDARD
EBS_GP3
EFS
S3_GLACIER

problems.title: S3 Lifecycle Policy Engine
problems.description: Implement a LifecyclePolicyEngine that applies a list of transition rules to an S3 object and returns its current storage class based on age. Rules are (afterDays, targetClass) pairs, sorted by age. The class returned is the last rule whose afterDays <= objectAge.
problems.hint1: Sort rules by afterDays ascending. Iterate through all rules and track the last applicable one.
problems.hint2: Start with STANDARD as the default. For each rule where rule.afterDays <= objectAge, update current class.
problems.hint3: Handle the case where no rules apply (object is newer than the smallest afterDays).

problems.title: Tiered Storage Manager — Hot/Warm/Cold Demotion
problems.description: Implement a TieredStorageManager that automatically demotes objects from HOT to WARM to COLD based on idle time. runTiering(nowMs) scans all objects and demotes any that have been idle beyond thresholds. access(key, nowMs) promotes an object back to HOT and updates lastAccessMs.
problems.hint1: Store objects in a Map<String, StorageObject> with lastAccessMs and tier fields.
problems.hint2: In runTiering: HOT idle >= hotToWarmMs → WARM. WARM idle >= warmToColdMs → COLD. Print demotion.
problems.hint3: In access: if tier != HOT, print promotion message, set tier=HOT. Always update lastAccessMs.
problems.testCases:
video.mp4 demoted to WARM
config.json demoted to WARM
video.mp4 demoted to COLD
config.json demoted to COLD
Promoted video.mp4 to HOT
video tier: HOT
config tier: COLD
